evidence_text: The study analyzes 40,214 pull requests: 6,618 human-authored PRs and 33,596 agentic PRs from the AIDev dataset.
citation: Yoshioka et al., MSR 2026, dataset composition
source_excerpt: "We first extract 64 features across 6 families from a dataset of 6,618 human-authored PRs and 33,596 agentic PRs from the AIDev dataset."
note: These counts describe the analyzed study sample, not all PRs in AIDev.
caveat: These counts describe the analyzed study sample, not all PRs in AIDev.

evidence_text: The authors extracted 64 features across six feature families, and one-hot encoding expanded the dataset to 106 model terms.
citation: Yoshioka et al., MSR 2026, feature extraction
source_excerpt: "We use our dataset of metadata to extract 64 features across six families..." and "After encoding all the categorical features with one-hot encoding, our dataset expanded to 106 model terms."
note: The paper points readers to an online appendix for the full feature list.
caveat: The paper points readers to an online appendix for the full feature list.

evidence_text: Variance Inflation Factor filtering removed 10 model terms, reducing the regression input from 106 to 96 terms.
citation: Yoshioka et al., MSR 2026, collinearity handling
source_excerpt: "Applying the commonly used threshold of VIF < 10... we eliminate ten model terms (two due to perfect collinearity and eight with VIF > 10), reducing the number of model terms across the feature families from 106 to 96."
note: This is a preprocessing step for model stability and interpretability.
caveat: This is a preprocessing step for model stability and interpretability.

evidence_text: The study fit two main logistic regression models on all human PRs and all agentic PRs, plus five agent-specific models for OpenAI Codex, GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Devin, and Claude Code.
citation: Yoshioka et al., MSR 2026, model set
source_excerpt: "First, we train two main models using the full datasets: one on all human PRs and one on all agentic PRs." and "Therefore, we further fit five additional models by partitioning the Agentic PR dataset by agent name and fitting a separate logistic regression model for each agent."
note: The model family is explicitly logistic regression.
caveat: The model family is explicitly logistic regression.

evidence_text: The human-PR logistic regression model achieved AUC 0.878, precision 0.859, recall 0.911, F1 0.884, and Brier score 0.119.
citation: Yoshioka et al., MSR 2026, human model performance
source_excerpt: In Table 2, the Human model reports: "AUC 0.878", "Precision 0.859", "Recall 0.911", "F1 0.884", and "Brier Score 0.119".
note: These metrics were evaluated via five-fold cross-validation.
caveat: These metrics were evaluated via five-fold cross-validation.

evidence_text: The all-agentic logistic regression model achieved AUC 0.960, precision 0.939, recall 0.909, F1 0.923, and Brier score 0.073.
citation: Yoshioka et al., MSR 2026, all-agentic model performance
source_excerpt: In Table 2, the All-agentic model reports: "AUC 0.960", "Precision 0.939", "Recall 0.909", "F1 0.923", and "Brier Score 0.073".
note: These metrics are reported as predictive and calibration measures, not causal estimates.
caveat: These metrics are reported as predictive and calibration measures, not causal estimates.

evidence_text: Submitter attributes were the most important feature family in both the human-PR and all-agentic-PR models, accounting for 69.00% and 76.15% of total LR chi-square, respectively.
citation: Yoshioka et al., MSR 2026, feature-family importance
source_excerpt: "Table 3 and Figure 2 show that submitter attributes dominate merge outcomes for both human PRs and agentic PRs, with relative importance of ... 69.00% and ... 76.15%, respectively."
note: The paper reports these percentages as relative explanatory power within each fitted model.
caveat: The paper reports these percentages as relative explanatory power within each fitted model.

evidence_text: When the contributor was also the integrator of the PR, the odds of merge increased by about 219 times for human PRs and 5,419 times for agentic PRs relative to cases with different contributor and integrator.
citation: Yoshioka et al., MSR 2026, same_user odds
source_excerpt: "A feature-level analysis of this family reveals that when the contributor is also the integrator of a PR... the odds of merging a PR increase by approximately 219 times for human PRs and by 5,419 times for agentic PRs, compared to cases where the contributor and integrator are different individuals."
note: This is an odds-based association from the fitted models.
caveat: This is an odds-based association from the fitted models.

evidence_text: Among merged PRs, 57.63% of human PRs and 77.51% of agentic PRs had same_user = 1, and the difference was significant in a two-proportion z-test (z = -29.38, p < 0.001).
citation: Yoshioka et al., MSR 2026, same_user merged proportions
source_excerpt: "We find that 57.63% of merged human PRs have same_user =1, compared to 77.51% of merged agentic PRs. This difference is also statistically significant (two-proportion z-test: z = −29.38, p < 0.001)."
note: This evidence reports observed merged-PR proportions plus a significance test.
caveat: This evidence reports observed merged-PR proportions plus a significance test.

evidence_text: Each additional reviewer comment was associated with a 2.7% increase in merge odds for human PRs but a 2.8% decrease in merge odds for agentic PRs.
citation: Yoshioka et al., MSR 2026, review comment effect contrast
source_excerpt: "For instance, each additional reviewer comment increases merge odds by 2.7% for human PRs, but decreases merge odds by 2.8% for agentic PRs."
note: This evidence is relevant because the same review signal points in opposite directions for human and agentic PRs, but the paper presents it as association rather than causal mechanism.
caveat: This evidence is relevant because the same review signal points in opposite directions for human and agentic PRs, but the paper presents it as association rather than causal mechanism.

evidence_text: For agentic PRs, 3.7% of merged PRs had more than three reviewers, compared with 5.8% of unmerged PRs.
citation: Yoshioka et al., MSR 2026, reviewer-count distribution
source_excerpt: "In fact, 3.7% of merged agentic PRs have more than three reviewers, whereas a higher percentage (i.e., 5.8%) of unmerged agentic PRs have more than three reviewers."
note: This is a descriptive contrast attached to the broader tension that heavier review activity aligns differently with merge outcomes in agentic versus human PRs.
caveat: This is a descriptive contrast attached to the broader tension that heavier review activity aligns differently with merge outcomes in agentic versus human PRs.

evidence_text: Across agent-specific models, OpenAI Codex PRs were most strongly associated with submitter features, Copilot PRs with PR change size and commit features, and Devin PRs with review and discussion features.
citation: Yoshioka et al., MSR 2026, agent-specific dominant families
source_excerpt: "Figure 3, the radar charts, shows that different agents’ PR merge likelihood is correlated with different feature families. In fact, OpenAI Codex’s most important feature family is submitter features... Copilot is primarily driven by PR change size and commit features, and Devin by review and discussion features."
note: The paper states this as comparative feature-family importance across agents.
caveat: The paper states this as comparative feature-family importance across agents.

evidence_text: A one-unit increase in number of commits linked to a PR was associated with 2.11 times higher merge likelihood for Copilot, 1.57 times higher merge likelihood for OpenAI Codex, and about a 7% decrease in merge odds for Devin.
citation: Yoshioka et al., MSR 2026, commit-count effect by agent
source_excerpt: "For example, within the PR change size and commit family, a unit increase in the number of commits linked to a PR increases merge likelihood by 2.11 times for Copilot and 1.57 times for OpenAI Codex, but is associated with an approximately 7% decrease in merge odds for Devin."
note: This evidence shows cross-agent tension in the direction of the same feature's association, without establishing why the differences occur.
caveat: This evidence shows cross-agent tension in the direction of the same feature's association, without establishing why the differences occur.

evidence_text: The study excluded Cursor and Claude Code from the RQ3 agent-comparison analysis because their models exceeded the degrees-of-freedom budget, with sample sizes of 1,541 and 459 PRs.
citation: Yoshioka et al., MSR 2026, RQ3 exclusions
source_excerpt: "Note that we exclude the logistic regression models for Cursor and Claude Code because they exceed the degrees-of-freedom budget..." and "Additionally, Cursor and Claude Code were excluded from RQ3 due to small sample sizes (N=1,541 and N=459)."
note: This bounds the scope of the agent-specific comparison results in RQ3.
caveat: This bounds the scope of the agent-specific comparison results in RQ3.

evidence_text: The authors state three validity limits: PRs with NULL merged_at include both closed and open PRs, the models identify correlations rather than causal relationships, and the AIDev dataset excludes repositories that prohibit AI-generated PRs.
citation: Yoshioka et al., MSR 2026, validity limits
source_excerpt: "We first extract 64 features across 6 families from a dataset of 6,618 human-authored PRs and 33,596 agentic PRs from the AIDev dataset."
note: These counts describe the analyzed study sample, not all PRs in AIDev.
caveat: These counts describe the analyzed study sample, not all PRs in AIDev.
